items.name: Complement
items.statement:
For any subset $A$ of $\mathbb{R}^d$, let $A^{\complement}$ denote the \textbf{complement} of $A$ in $\mathbb{R}^d$, i.e.,
$$A^{\complement} = \mathbb{R}^d \setminus A = \{\boldsymbol{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d \mid \boldsymbol{x} \notin A\}.$$

items.name: Open and Closed Sets
items.statement: A subset $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$ is said to be \textbf{open} if, for each point $\boldsymbol{x} \in A$, there is an $r > 0$ such that $B_r(\boldsymbol{x}) \subseteq A$, and to be \textbf{closed} if $A^{\complement}$ is open.

items.name: Interior Point
items.statement: A point $\boldsymbol{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is said to be an \textbf{interior point} of a subset $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$ if there is an $r > 0$ such that $B_r(\boldsymbol{x}) \subseteq A$. By $\operatorname{int}(A)$ we denote the set of interior points of $A$, which is the \textbf{interior} of $A$.
A set $A$ is open if and only if every point of $A$ is an interior point of $A$. Equivalently, $A$ is open if and only if $\operatorname{int}(A) = A$.

(i) The union of any number of open subsets of $\mathbb{R}^d$ is open.
(ii) The intersection of any finite number of open subsets of $\mathbb{R}^d$ is open.
(iii) The union of any finite number of closed subsets of $\mathbb{R}^d$ is closed.
(iv) The intersection of any number of closed subsets of $\mathbb{R}^d$ is closed.

items.name: Bolzano-Weierstrass Theorem
items.statement: If $A$ is a bounded infinite subset of $\mathbb{R}^d$, then there is a point $\boldsymbol{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ with the property that the set $A \cap B_r(\boldsymbol{x})$ is infinite for each $r > 0$.

items.name: Cluster Point / Derived Set
items.statement: A point $\boldsymbol{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is said to be a \textbf{cluster point} of a subset $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$ if $A \cap \widehat{B}_r(\boldsymbol{x}) \neq \emptyset$ for all $r > 0$; let $\operatorname{der}(A)$ denote the set of all cluster points of $A$, which is called the \textbf{derived set} of $A$.

items.name: Sequence in a Set
items.statement: A \textbf{sequence in} a set $A$ is a sequence, all of whose terms belong to the set $A$.

If $\boldsymbol{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and if $A$ is a subset of $\mathbb{R}^d$, the following three conditions are equivalent:
(i) $\boldsymbol{x}$ is a cluster point of $A$;
(ii) $A \cap B_r(\boldsymbol{x})$ is infinite for each $r > 0$.
(iii) there is a sequence $(\boldsymbol{x}_n)$ in $A$ such that $\boldsymbol{x}_m \neq \boldsymbol{x}_n$ whenever $m \neq n$ and $\lim_{n \to \infty} \boldsymbol{x}_n = \boldsymbol{x}$.

items.name: Limit Point / Closure
items.statement: A point $\boldsymbol{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is said to be a \textbf{limit point} of a subset $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$ if $A \cap B_r(\boldsymbol{x}) \neq \emptyset$ for all $r > 0$; let $\operatorname{cl}(A)$ denote the set of all limit points of $A$, which is called the \textbf{closure} of $A$.

items.name: Isolated Point
items.statement: A point $\boldsymbol{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is said to be an \textbf{isolated point} of a subset $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$ if there exists $r > 0$ such that $A \cap B_r(\boldsymbol{x}) = \{\boldsymbol{x}\}$.
For a subset $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$, a limit point $\boldsymbol{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is a cluster point for $A$ if and only if it is not an isolated point of $A$.

items.name: Boundary Point
items.statement: A point $\boldsymbol{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is said to be a \textbf{boundary point} of a subset $A$ of $\mathbb{R}^d$ if, for each $r > 0$, the ball $B_r(\boldsymbol{x})$ contains at least one point in $A$ and at least one point in $A^{\complement}$; let $\operatorname{bd}(A)$ denote the set of boundary points of the set $A$.
For a subset $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$ we have $\operatorname{cl}(A) = A \cup \operatorname{bd}(A)$.

items.name: Compact
items.statement: A subset $K \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$ is \textbf{compact} if it is closed and bounded.

items.name: Extreme Value Theorem
items.statement:
Suppose that $A$ is a compact subset of $\mathbb{R}^d$ and that $\boldsymbol{f}$ is a continuous $\mathbb{R}^m$-valued function with domain $A$.
(i) The image $\operatorname{image}(\boldsymbol{f}) = \{f(\boldsymbol{x}) \mid \boldsymbol{x} \in A\}$ of $\boldsymbol{f}$ is a compact subset of $\mathbb{R}^m$.
(ii) If $m = 1$, then $f$ attains a minimum value and a maximum value on $A$, i.e., there are points $\boldsymbol{u}$ and $\boldsymbol{v}$ in $A$ such that
$$f(\boldsymbol{u}) \leq f(\boldsymbol{x}) \leq f(\boldsymbol{v})$$
for all $\boldsymbol{x} \in A$.

items.name: Open Covering / Finite Subcover
items.statement:
Suppose that $A$ is a subset of $\mathbb{R}^d$. A set $\mathcal{U}$ of subsets of $\mathbb{R}^d$ is said
(i) to be an \textbf{open covering} of $A$ if each set in $\mathcal{U}$ is open and if $A \subseteq \bigcup_{U \in \mathcal{U}} U$, and
(ii) to have a \textbf{finite subcover} if there are $U_1, \ldots, U_n$ in $\mathcal{U}$ such that $A \subseteq \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} U_i$.

items.name: Heine-Borel Theorem
Uniformly Continuous
items.statement: Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$ and let $f$ be a real-valued function on $A$. The function $f$ is \textbf{uniformly continuous} if, for each $\epsilon > 0$, there exists $\delta > 0$ such that $|f(\boldsymbol{x}) - f(\boldsymbol{y})| < \epsilon$ if $\boldsymbol{x}, \boldsymbol{y} \in A$ satisfy $\|\boldsymbol{x} - \boldsymbol{y}\| < \delta$.

items.name: Heine-Cantor Theorem
items.statement: If $K \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$ is compact, then a real-valued function $f$ on $K$ is continuous if and only if it is uniformly continuous.
If $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$ is compact and if $\boldsymbol{f}$ is a continuous $\mathbb{R}^m$-valued function on $A$, then $\operatorname{image}(\boldsymbol{f})$ is compact.

items.name: Infinite Series / Partial Sums
items.statement:
Consider a sequence $(a_k)$ of real numbers defined for $k \geq 1$. The formal expression
$$\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} a_k$$
is called the \textbf{infinite series} corresponding to the sequence $(a_k)$. For $n \in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}$ the sum
$$S_n = \sum_{k=1}^{n} a_k$$
is called the $n$ \textbf{partial sum} of (5.1) and the sequence $(S_n)$ is called the \textbf{sequence of partial sums} corresponding to (5.1).

If the sequence $(S_n)$ converges then the series (5.1) is said to \textbf{converge} and one says that the value of $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} a_k$ is the limit $\lim_{n \to \infty} S_n$, and one writes $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} a_k = \lim_{n \to \infty} S_n$. On the other hand, if the sequence $(S_n)$ diverges, then the series (5.1) is said to \textbf{diverge} or to \textbf{not exist} and no meaning is assigned to the symbol $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} a_k$.

items.name: Divergence Test
items.statement:
Let $(a_k)$ be a sequence of numbers.
(i) If the infinite series $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} a_k$ converges, then $\lim_{k \to \infty} a_k = 0$.
(ii) If the sequence $(a_k)$ does not converge to $0$, then the infinite series $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} a_k$ diverges.
If $(a_k)$ is a sequence of nonnegative terms, then the infinite series $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} a_k$ converges if and only if the corresponding sequence of partial sums is bounded.

items.name: Comparison Test
items.statement:
Suppose that $(a_k)$ and $(b_k)$ are two sequences.
(i) If $0 \leq a_k \leq b_k$ for all $k$ and if the series $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} b_k$ converges, then so does the series $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} a_k$.
(ii) If $0 \leq a_k \leq b_k$ for all $k$ and if the series $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} a_k$ diverges, then so does the series $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} b_k$.
(iii) If $b_k > 0$ for all $k$, if $0 \leq \lim_{k \to \infty} a_k/b_k < \infty$, and if $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} b_k$ converges, then so does $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} a_k$.
(iv) If $b_k > 0$ for all $k$, if $0 < \lim_{k \to \infty} a_k/b_k \leq \infty$, and if $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} b_k$ diverges, then so does $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} a_k$.

items.name: Integral Test for Positive Series
items.statement:
Suppose that $(a_k)$ is a sequence and that $f$ is a function which is continuous, nonnegative-valued, and nonincreasing on the half-line $[1, \infty)$, and satisfies $a_k = f(k)$ for all $k \in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}$. Then the infinite series $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} a_k$ and the improper integral
$$\int_1^{\infty} f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x$$
either both converge or both diverge.

items.name: Ratio Test for Positive Series
items.statement:
Suppose that $(a_k)$ is a sequence of positive terms and assume that $\lim_{k \to \infty} \frac{a_{k+1}}{a_k} = L$ exists.
(i) If $L < 1$, then $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} a_k$ converges.
(ii) If $L > 1$, then $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} a_k$ diverges.
(iii) If $L = 1$, then this test is inconclusive.

items.name: Root Test for Positive Series
items.statement:
Suppose that $(a_n)$ is a sequence of positive terms and let $L = \limsup_{n \to \infty} \sqrt[n]{a_n}$.
(i) If $L < 1$ then $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n$ converges.
(ii) If $L > 1$ then $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n$ diverges.
(iii) If $L = 1$ this test is inconclusive.
$\lim_{n \to \infty} \sqrt[n]{n} = 1$.

items.name: Absolute Convergence Implies Convergence
items.statement: If $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} |a_n|$ converges, then so does $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n$.

items.name: Absolute and Conditional Convergence
items.statement:
The series $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n$ is said
(i) to \textbf{converge absolutely} if the series $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} |a_n|$ converges and
(ii) to \textbf{converge conditionally} if $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n$ converges but $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} |a_n|$ diverges.

items.name: Integral Test for General Series
items.statement:
Suppose that $(a_k)$ is a sequence and that $f$ is a function which is continuous, nonnegative-valued, and nonincreasing on the half-line $[1, \infty)$, and satisfies $|a_k| = f(k)$ for all $k \in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}$. Then the infinite series $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} a_k$ converges if the improper integral
$$\int_1^{\infty} f(x)\,\mathrm{d}x$$
converges.

items.name: Ratio Test for General Series
items.statement:
Suppose that $(a_k)$ is a sequence of nonzero terms and assume that $\lim_{k \to \infty} |\frac{a_{k+1}}{a_k}| = L$ exists.
(i) If $L < 1$, then $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} a_k$ converges.
(ii) If $L > 1$, then $\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} a_k$ diverges.
(iii) If $L = 1$, then this test is inconclusive.

items.name: Root Test for General Series
items.statement:
Suppose that $(a_n)$ is a sequence of nonzero terms and let $L = \limsup_{n \to \infty} \sqrt[n]{|a_n|}$.
(i) If $L < 1$ then $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n$ converges.
(ii) If $L > 1$ then $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n$ diverges.
(iii) If $L = 1$ this test is inconclusive.

items.name: Alternating Series Test
items.statement:
If $(a_n)$ is a sequence such that
(i) $\lim_{n \to \infty} a_n = 0$,
(ii) $|a_{n+1}| < |a_n|$ for all $n$, and
(iii) $a_{n+1}/a_n < 0$ for all $n$,
then the series $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n$ converges.

items.name: Riemann Rearrangement Theorem
items.statement:
By permuting the terms of a conditionally convergent series we can
(i) make it converge to any number we wish or diverge to $\pm\infty$ or
(ii) make its partial sums oscillate between any two numbers.
